context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class about neoclassical vs dependency theories. I’m pretty unsure how to answer the part about whether planning is necessary and how dependency theory connects to current critiques of the WTO and “policy space.”
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've identified the right moving parts. Let's start from the beginning: what do you already know about what neoclassical economists say about how development happens? What drives growth in their view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and has identified the key parts of the prompt. Rather than scaffolding everything at once, I should ask them to share what they already know to assess their starting point and let them do the intellectual work. I'll focus on one part of the prompt at a time, starting with the most foundational piece: what neoclassical economists say about development and planning.

exchanges.student: I think neoclassical economists mostly say growth comes from markets working efficiently, with investment, trade, and maybe technology increasing productivity. I’m a little fuzzy on whether they assume the state should just stay out of it, or if there’s still some role for planning.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to notice that tension. Think about it this way: if markets are assumed to be efficient and self-correcting, what does that logically imply about the need for state planning or intervention?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a decent grasp of the basics of neoclassical economics. Their uncertainty about the state's role is exactly the right tension to explore. I should push them to think this through rather than resolve it for them. A targeted Socratic question about what 'efficient markets' imply about intervention should help them work it out.

exchanges.student: Then I guess it implies planning isn’t seen as very necessary, because prices and competition are supposed to allocate resources better than the state. Maybe the state just sets basic rules and infrastructure, but not heavy direction of the economy?
exchanges.tutor: That's solid reasoning. Now, how does dependency theory see the world differently? What's their diagnosis of why poor countries stay poor?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned through the neoclassical position well on their own. I should affirm the logic without giving away too much, and now move them toward the dependency theory side of the prompt, which is the next key piece they need to work through.

exchanges.student: I think dependency theory says poor countries stay poor because they’re structurally tied to rich countries in a way that benefits the core and hurts the periphery. So underdevelopment isn’t just an early stage before growth—it’s kind of produced by the global system itself.
exchanges.tutor: Good. So if underdevelopment is produced by the global system, what does that imply about what dependency theorists would say about planning as a tool? Would they reject it, or might they actually see a role for it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has captured the core dependency theory argument well. Now I should push them toward the logical implication for planning, which is what the prompt directly asks. This is a good moment to let them reason through whether dependency theorists would be for or against planning, given their diagnosis.

exchanges.student: Then they’d probably see a bigger role for planning, not reject it. I think they’d want the state to use planning to reduce dependence, protect domestic industry, and control how the country fits into the world economy.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now, with that in mind, think about what 'policy space' means — the idea that developing countries should have room to use industrial policy, tariffs, subsidies, etc. How might that connect to what dependency theorists were arguing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned that dependency theorists are not hostile to planning - in fact they advocate for it. This directly answers one of the prompt's questions. Now I should move them to the final and most complex part of the prompt: the connection between dependency theory and current critiques of the WTO and 'policy space'. This is where the reflection can get sophisticated.

exchanges.student: It sounds pretty connected, honestly. “Policy space” seems like the idea that poorer countries shouldn’t be forced into one free-market model by global institutions, because they need freedom to protect and build their own economies first. That feels a lot like the dependency critique of unequal global rules, even if the language is newer.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation. One follow-up: can you think of any specific institutions or moments where this tension between 'free-market rules' and 'policy space' has actually played out? Having a concrete example would strengthen your reflection considerably.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong and insightful connection between dependency theory and the policy space argument. This is the key insight the prompt is looking for. Now is a good moment to push them slightly further to think about who is making these arguments today and in what context, to add concreteness to their reflection. I should also note we've now covered all four parts of the prompt and could nudge them toward writing.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking maybe the WTO rules around tariffs and subsidies, or structural adjustment from the IMF and World Bank, even though that’s not exactly the WTO. I’m not totally sure which example fits best, but the general tension seems to be that developing countries get pushed to liberalize while richer countries historically used protection and industrial policy themselves.
exchanges.tutor: That double standard you identified — rich countries industrialized behind protective walls, then pushed liberalization on others — is a really powerful point. That's exactly the kind of echo the prompt is asking about. You now have all the building blocks. Can you try drafting your opening paragraph, connecting neoclassical thinking to the question of whether planning is necessary?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has hit on a very important and sophisticated point - the historical double standard (rich countries used protection to develop, now they push liberalization on poorer countries). This is sometimes called 'kicking away the ladder' and is central to current policy space debates. I should affirm this and push them to connect it explicitly back to the dependency framework in preparation for writing. We've now covered all parts of the prompt well across several exchanges, so I should encourage them to start drafting.

exchanges.student:
Okay, maybe something like: Neoclassical economists generally view development as the result of efficient markets, investment, trade, and productivity growth. In this framework, planning is not central because markets are assumed to allocate resources more effectively than the state. The role of government is usually limited to maintaining basic institutions and conditions for markets to function, rather than directing development through strong planning.

Is that too simplistic?
exchanges.tutor: It's a good, clear start — not too simplistic for an opening paragraph. One thing to consider: is there any nuance within neoclassical thinking you might be flattening? For instance, do all neoclassical economists agree on how minimal the state's role should be? That said, don't over-refine this paragraph — keep moving and try drafting the dependency theory section next.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid opening paragraph that accurately captures the neoclassical view. It's not too simplistic - it's appropriately concise for an intro paragraph. I should give honest, constructive feedback without being falsely encouraging. I should push them to add a bit more nuance or depth rather than just validating, and remind them that grading is not my role. Then move them forward to the next section.

exchanges.student:
Yeah, I’m probably flattening it a bit. I guess I can say planning is generally not central in neoclassical models, even if the state still has some limited role.  

For the dependency part: Dependency theorists reject the idea that all countries follow the same path to development through market integration. They argue that poor countries remain underdeveloped because they are inserted into a global economic system structured to benefit wealthy countries. From this perspective, planning is not rejected at all; instead, it can be a necessary tool for reducing external dependence, protecting domestic industries, and giving the state more control over national development.
exchanges.tutor: That dependency paragraph is good — it directly answers the prompt's question. Now the most important move is connecting this to the WTO and policy space. Try drafting that final section, using the double-standard point you identified earlier. That's where your reflection can really come together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress. The dependency paragraph is solid and directly addresses the prompt's question about whether dependency theorists are hostile to planning. I should give honest feedback and push them toward the final section connecting dependency theory to current WTO critiques and policy space, which is the most complex and sophisticated part of the reflection. We are nearing the end of the planned conversation length so I should encourage them to write the final section.

exchanges.student: Okay, here’s my try: Current criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO echo dependency theory in arguing that global economic rules are not neutral. Critics of the WTO and advocates of “policy space” argue that developing countries should be allowed to use tariffs, subsidies, and industrial policies to build their economies, rather than being locked into free-trade rules that mainly benefit already industrialized countries. This argument reflects dependency theory’s concern with unequal power in the global economy and the way international institutions can reproduce that inequality. A major irony is that many rich countries used protectionist and interventionist policies during their own development, but later promoted liberalization for poorer countries.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong paragraph — the irony point lands well and the connection to dependency theory is clear. One thing to reflect on: does your piece currently explain *why* policy space advocates think this matters practically for planning? You identify the double standard, but what's the stakes for a developing country today? Check your word count and read the whole piece as one — does it flow as a coherent argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a strong final section that ties together dependency theory, WTO critiques, and the policy space argument with a compelling irony point. This is genuinely good work. I should give honest, constructive feedback, point out where it's strong, and suggest one area for potential refinement without writing anything for them. I should also remind them to check their word count and make sure the full reflection holds together coherently.
